title: Enhanced Valley Polarization via Nonlinear Cascaded Quantum-Geometric Selection Rules
authors: Quentin Courtade, Sotirios Fragkos, Dominique Descamps, Stéphane Petit, Yann Mairesse, Michael Schüler, Samuel Beaulieu
abstract: The quantum geometric properties of Bloch electrons fundamentally govern light-matter interactions and optical selection rules in solids. In semiconducting transition-metal dichalcogenides, circularly polarized excitation near the band edge enables valley-selective interband transitions, providing the basis for valleytronics. While nonlinear optical protocols are being developed to manipulate and probe valley selection rules, they largely rely on band-edge transitions that proceed via virtual intermediate states. Here, we demonstrate a doubly resonant cascaded nonlinear pathway from the valence band to high-lying states, mediated by a real intermediate state whose participation substantially reshapes the valley optical selection rules. Using time- and angle-resolved extreme-ultraviolet photoemission spectroscopy in combination with a time-dependent Lindblad master-equation formalism, we show that this cascaded nonlinear photoexcitation produces a substantially enhanced high-lying valley polarization compared to the conventional linear optical response near the band edge. The extension of the quantum-geometry-based selection rules to the nonlinear regime and high-lying bands offers new perspectives for ultrafast valleytronics and should play a determinant role in strong-field-driven phenomena in quantum materials.
summary: This paper shows that using a nonlinear, two-photon cascaded process in MoTe2 can create much stronger valley polarization in high-energy electronic states than standard single-photon methods. By leveraging a real intermediate state, the researchers found they could effectively 'double' the chiroptical selection rules. This discovery provides a new pathway for controlling valley degrees of freedom in ultrafast valleytronics.
main_problem: The study aims to understand how to enhance valley polarization in high-lying conduction bands of transition-metal dichalcogenides beyond the limits of conventional linear optical response.
main_result: The researchers demonstrate that a doubly resonant, cascaded nonlinear pathway via a real intermediate state significantly enhances valley polarization in high-lying states compared to the first conduction band.
method: The study combines time- and angle-resolved extreme-ultraviolet photoemission spectroscopy (trARPES) with a time-dependent Lindblad master-equation formalism and DFT calculations.
model_or_system: The physical system is monolayer 2H-MoTe2, modeled as a three-level ladder-type electronic structure (valence band, first conduction band, and a high-lying conduction band) using a Lindblad master equation to account for dynamics and dissipation.
key_observables: Energy- and momentum-resolved photoemission intensity, band-resolved local orbital angular momentum (Lz), and valley asymmetry (normalized difference between LCP and RCP pump intensities).
important_parameters: Pump wavelength (1.2 eV), XUV probe energy (21.6 eV), carrier lifetimes (ranging from ~47 fs to ~1.4 ps), and peak electric field strength (1.6 MV/cm).
main_assumptions: The model assumes a three-level ladder system and uses a Lindblad formalism to approximate dissipative decay and intervalley scattering.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the experimental setup and orbital angular momentum textures; Figure 2 presents energy-momentum maps, time-resolved intensities, and Lindblad simulations; Figure 3 displays momentum distribution curves for LCP and RCP pumps.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of valley polarization enhancement, describes the experimental trARPES setup and theoretical Lindblad framework, presents the observation of enhanced polarization in high-lying states, and provides a microscopic explanation based on cascaded selection rules.
why_interesting: The use of a Lindblad master equation to model nonlinear, multi-photon light-matter interactions and the exploration of quantum-geometric selection rules in a driven-dissipative regime are highly relevant to researchers in quantum optics and open quantum systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
